_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against 2019/2020 AAD-NPF psoriasis guidelines, 2021 ACR-NPF psoriatic arthritis guideline, UNCOVER-1/2/3, VOYAGE-1/2, MEASURE UP, and BE-VIVID trial primary literature; prevalence figures from CDC/NPF 2021 and Global Burden of Disease 2019.

h1Title: Psoriasis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Psoriasis is a chronic immune-mediated inflammatory disease in which IL-23 and IL-17 driven activation of T cells accelerates the skin's renewal cycle from roughly 28 days to 3-5 days, producing the characteristic thick, scaly red plaques. It affects about 7.5 million US adults (3% of the population) and roughly 125 million people worldwide, with onset peaks between ages 15-25 and 50-60. Plaque psoriasis accounts for around 90% of cases and typically involves the scalp, elbows, knees, and lower back; one in three patients develops psoriatic arthritis. Psoriasis is now understood as a systemic disease — patients carry higher risk of cardiovascular events, metabolic syndrome, and depression — not just a skin condition. Modern biologic therapies targeting IL-17 and IL-23 deliver complete or near-complete skin clearance in 60-85% of patients within 16 weeks, transforming what was once a lifelong burden into a controllable disease.

keyStats.prevalence: ~3% of US adults (7.5 million); ~125 million people worldwide
keyStats.demographics: Affects men and women equally; less common in East Asian, African, and Indigenous populations than in European-descent populations
keyStats.avgAge: Bimodal onset: peaks at 15-25 (Type I, HLA-Cw6 associated) and 50-60 (Type II)
keyStats.globalCases: ~125 million people; rising prevalence linked to obesity and metabolic syndrome

definition: Psoriasis (ICD-10: L40; L40.0 plaque, L40.1 generalized pustular, L40.4 guttate, L40.5 arthropathic, L40.9 unspecified) is a chronic immune-mediated inflammatory disease of the skin and joints driven by the IL-23/Th17 axis. Activated dendritic cells release IL-23, which expands Th17 cells producing IL-17A, IL-17F, IL-22, and TNF-α; these cytokines drive keratinocyte hyperproliferation, abnormal differentiation, and neutrophilic infiltration of the epidermis. The pathognomonic clinical lesion is a well-demarcated, erythematous plaque with silvery-white scale, typically symmetric and favoring extensor surfaces. Disease severity is staged by body surface area involvement and validated indices (PASI for plaque psoriasis, BSA, IGA), with absolute PASI 90 or PASI 100 — 90% or 100% reduction from baseline — now the modern benchmark of effective therapy. Psoriasis is currently understood as a systemic inflammatory disease with increased risk of psoriatic arthritis (in 30% of skin-disease patients), cardiometabolic disease, inflammatory bowel disease, uveitis, depression, and substance use. Dermatologists are the primary specialty; rheumatologists co-manage psoriatic arthritis; cardiology referral is increasingly recommended for severe disease.

typesClassification.description: Accounts for ~90% of cases. Well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery scale, typically on scalp, elbows, knees, lower back, and umbilicus. Chronic, slowly relapsing course.

Sudden eruption of small drop-like papules across the trunk and proximal extremities, often 1-3 weeks after streptococcal pharyngitis. More common in children and young adults; may resolve or transition to plaque psoriasis.
Smooth, shiny, erythematous plaques without scale in skin folds — axillae, inguinal creases, intergluteal cleft, inframammary folds. Often misdiagnosed as fungal infection.
Sterile pustules on erythematous skin. Generalized pustular psoriasis (von Zumbusch) is a medical emergency with fever and systemic inflammation; palmoplantar pustulosis is a localized variant.
Generalized erythema covering ≥75-90% of body surface area with shedding of large sheets of scale. Causes temperature and fluid dysregulation; requires hospitalization.
Affects 50% of psoriasis patients and 80% of those with psoriatic arthritis. Pitting, onycholysis, oil-drop discoloration, and subungual hyperkeratosis. Strongly associated with PsA.
Thick, well-demarcated scaly plaques on the scalp, often extending beyond the hairline onto the forehead, postauricular skin, and neck. Affects ~50% of patients.

primarySymptoms: Well-demarcated, raised red plaques covered with thick silvery-white scale, classically on the elbows, knees, scalp, and lower back; lesions are symmetric and persist for months to years.
Pinpoint bleeding when scale is scraped off — the Auspitz sign — caused by tortuous dilated capillaries in the dermal papillae just beneath the thinned epidermis.
Itching of moderate intensity in roughly 70-90% of patients; less prominent than in eczema but often interferes with sleep.
Burning, stinging, or soreness within active plaques, especially in flexural psoriasis or sensitive sites like the genitals.
Cracking of plaque skin with painful fissures, particularly over the knuckles, palms, and soles; bleeding fissures are common in palmoplantar disease.
Nail changes including pitting (small depressions in the nail plate), onycholysis (lifting of the nail), oil-drop spots, and subungual hyperkeratosis — present in 50% of patients.
Scalp involvement with thick, scaly plaques that extend beyond the hairline onto the forehead, retroauricular skin, and posterior neck; temporary diffuse hair shedding can occur.
Koebner phenomenon — new plaques developing 7-14 days after skin trauma, surgical incisions, tattoos, or sunburn — present in 25-30% of patients.
Joint pain, stiffness, and swelling (especially morning stiffness lasting >30 minutes), dactylitis (sausage digits), and enthesitis — features of psoriatic arthritis in 30% of skin-disease patients.
Psychological burden including depression, anxiety, and reduced quality of life that often exceeds the burden of comparable medical conditions; suicidality risk is elevated.
earlyWarningSigns: A small scaly red patch on the elbow, knee, or scalp that does not respond to over-the-counter moisturizer over 2-3 weeks
New nail pitting or onycholysis without nail trauma or fungal infection
A guttate eruption — sudden small papules over the trunk in the 1-3 weeks after a sore throat
Persistent dandruff that extends beyond the hairline onto the forehead or behind the ears
Joint stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes each morning in a patient with known skin disease — early psoriatic arthritis
emergencySigns: Sudden generalized erythema with sheet-like scaling, fever, and chills — erythrodermic psoriasis with risk of temperature dysregulation and high-output cardiac failure
Widespread sterile pustules with fever and elevated white count — generalized pustular psoriasis; admit for IV fluids, monitoring, and acute systemic therapy
Severe new joint swelling with inability to bear weight or use the hand — acute psoriatic arthritis flare requiring urgent rheumatology evaluation
Suicidal thoughts or severe depression in a psoriasis patient — psoriasis carries elevated suicide risk; urgent mental-health evaluation
Acute red eye with photophobia in a psoriasis patient — possible uveitis, especially with comorbid psoriatic arthritis; ophthalmology evaluation within 24 hours

causes.description: Heritability is roughly 60-70%. The strongest locus is PSORS1 on chromosome 6p21, with HLA-Cw0602 the dominant risk allele (especially in early-onset disease). Variants in IL23R, IL12B, TNFAIP3, and CARD14 further increase susceptibility.

The core mechanism. Plasmacytoid and myeloid dendritic cells in the skin release IL-23, expanding Th17 cells and driving IL-17A, IL-17F, IL-22, and TNF-α production. These cytokines stimulate keratinocyte hyperproliferation and recruit neutrophils, creating the visible plaque.

Group A streptococcal pharyngitis precedes 60-80% of guttate psoriasis episodes and can trigger flares of plaque disease. Antistreptococcal antibody titers are typically elevated in the weeks following onset.

Scratches, surgical incisions, tattoos, sunburns, and friction sites develop new plaques 7-14 days later in roughly 25-30% of patients. Demonstrates the role of innate immune activation by damage-associated molecular patterns.

Lithium, beta-blockers, antimalarials, NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, and abrupt withdrawal of systemic corticosteroids can precipitate or worsen psoriasis. Severity ranges from mild flares to triggering erythrodermic or pustular disease.
Acute stressful life events precede flares in 40-80% of patients. Stress-induced glucocorticoid and neuropeptide signaling alters cutaneous immune function. Chronic stress reduces treatment response.

Adipose tissue is an active source of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6). Obesity increases incidence and severity of psoriasis and reduces response to systemic and biologic therapy; weight loss measurably improves disease control.

Smoking doubles psoriasis incidence and is strongly associated with palmoplantar pustular psoriasis. Heavy alcohol use raises severity and worsens treatment response, particularly with methotrexate (hepatotoxicity).

riskFactors.description: Roughly 40% of patients have an affected first-degree relative. If one parent has psoriasis the child has a ~16% risk; if both parents are affected the risk approaches 50%.
Carriers have a 9-23-fold increased risk of early-onset (Type I) psoriasis and a stronger response to IL-23 inhibitors in some studies.
BMI ≥30 raises psoriasis risk by roughly 50% and worsens severity; bariatric surgery and 10% weight loss measurably improve PASI scores.
Active smokers have ~2× the incidence of psoriasis; palmoplantar pustulosis is strongly tobacco-associated. Cessation reduces severity over months.
More than 80 g alcohol per week is associated with more severe and treatment-resistant psoriasis, especially in men.
Group A strep pharyngitis can trigger guttate psoriasis and flare plaque disease; tonsillectomy has been reported to reduce severity in selected refractory cases.
Major stressful life events and untreated depression worsen flares and reduce treatment response; integrated mental-health support improves outcomes.
Drug-triggered psoriasis can present as new disease or worsening of existing disease; review medications carefully at every visit.
Many patients note seasonal worsening in winter due to dry skin and reduced UV exposure; humidification and phototherapy partially mitigate.
Psoriasis can present de novo or worsen severely in HIV; antiretroviral therapy often improves the skin disease through immune reconstitution.

affectedDemographics: US prevalence: 7.5 million adults (~3% of the adult population), per NPF/CDC 2021 estimates
European prevalence: 1.5-3% across most countries; rates lowest in Asia and parts of Africa
Affects men and women approximately equally
Bimodal onset peaks at ages 15-25 (Type I, HLA-Cw6 positive) and 50-60 (Type II)
Lower reported prevalence in East Asian (~0.5%), African (~0.5-1%), and Indigenous populations, partly genetic and partly ascertainment-related
diagnosisOverview: Psoriasis is a clinical diagnosis. Most cases are recognized on a bedside examination of well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery scale over typical extensor surfaces, scalp, umbilicus, and intergluteal cleft. The Auspitz sign (pinpoint bleeding after scale removal) and Koebner phenomenon (new lesions at sites of trauma) further support the diagnosis. The exam should always include the scalp, nails (pitting, onycholysis, oil-drop sign), gluteal cleft, umbilicus, and palms and soles — sites patients often do not show. Skin biopsy is reserved for atypical presentations or when distinguishing psoriasis from mycosis fungoides, subacute cutaneous lupus, or pityriasis rubra pilaris. Histology shows parakeratosis, neutrophilic Munro microabscesses, elongated rete ridges, and capillary dilation in the dermal papillae. Severity is assessed by body surface area, Psoriasis Area and Severity Index (PASI), Investigator Global Assessment (IGA), and quality-of-life metrics such as the Dermatology Life Quality Index (DLQI). Joint assessment is mandatory because 30% of patients develop psoriatic arthritis; the PsA Screening and Evaluation (PASE) and PEST questionnaires identify at-risk patients for rheumatology referral. Baseline labs include CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel, fasting lipid panel, hepatitis B/C screening, tuberculosis testing (PPD or interferon-gamma release assay), and pregnancy testing in women of reproductive age prior to systemic therapy. Cardiometabolic screening — blood pressure, fasting glucose, lipid panel — is recommended at diagnosis given the established cardiovascular risk.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Recognizes the classic morphology, distribution, and Auspitz/Koebner signs. Confirms diagnosis in most plaque psoriasis cases without further testing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Visual inspection of the whole skin surface, scalp, nails, and intertriginous sites by the dermatologist. Takes 10-15 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms diagnosis in atypical cases or when ruling out mycosis fungoides, subacute cutaneous lupus, or pityriasis rubra pilaris. Histology shows parakeratosis, Munro microabscesses, and elongated rete ridges.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 3-4 mm circular skin sample taken under local anesthesia. One or two sutures, healing over 1-2 weeks. Mild discomfort.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies severity from 0-72 by scoring erythema, induration, scale, and area in four body regions. PASI 75/90/100 (75%, 90%, 100% reduction from baseline) are standard treatment response benchmarks.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed by the clinician during examination. No additional testing — adds 5 minutes to the visit.

diagnosticTests.test: Joint examination plus PEST or PASE questionnaire
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for psoriatic arthritis. Joint tenderness, swelling, enthesitis, dactylitis, and morning stiffness >30 minutes prompt rheumatology referral.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A short questionnaire plus joint examination during the dermatology visit.

diagnosticTests.test: Baseline labs prior to systemic therapy
diagnosticTests.purpose: CBC, CMP, LFTs, fasting lipid panel, hepatitis B/C serology, tuberculosis screening (IGRA or PPD), and pregnancy testing as appropriate. Establishes eligibility and safety baseline for systemic and biologic agents.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw plus, in some cases, a TB skin test that requires a return visit 48-72 hours later for reading.

diagnosticTests.test: Imaging for psoriatic arthritis (X-ray, ultrasound, MRI)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Plain radiographs detect erosions, pencil-in-cup deformity, and joint space narrowing. Ultrasound and MRI detect early enthesitis and sacroiliitis when X-rays are normal.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: X-ray: a few minutes per affected joint. MRI: 30-45 minutes lying still in a closed-bore magnet; contrast may be used.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is matched to severity, comorbidities, and patient preference. Mild disease (BSA <3-5%) is generally managed with topical corticosteroids, vitamin D analogues (calcipotriene, calcitriol), combination products (calcipotriene-betamethasone foam/cream), topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, pimecrolimus, especially for face and folds), and emollients. Moderate to severe disease (BSA ≥5-10%, involvement of high-impact sites like face/genitals/palms, or significant quality-of-life impairment) generally requires phototherapy or systemic therapy. Narrowband UVB (311-313 nm) phototherapy 2-3 times weekly for 8-12 weeks induces remission in 60-75% of patients but requires committed clinic visits. Traditional oral systemics include methotrexate (15-25 mg weekly with folic acid), cyclosporine (2.5-5 mg/kg/day for short-term flare control), and acitretin (10-50 mg daily; teratogenic for years after stopping). Apremilast (PDE-4 inhibitor, 30 mg twice daily) is an oral option with a modest response rate but a clean monitoring profile. Biologic therapy has transformed care. IL-17 inhibitors (secukinumab, ixekizumab, brodalumab, bimekizumab) and IL-23 inhibitors (guselkumab, risankizumab, tildrakizumab) achieve PASI 90 in 60-85% of patients by week 16; IL-12/23 inhibitor ustekinumab and TNF inhibitors (adalimumab, etanercept, infliximab, certolizumab) remain important. Bimekizumab (anti-IL-17A/F) achieves PASI 100 in roughly 60% at week 16. Oral TYK2 inhibitor deucravacitinib (6 mg daily) offers a pill alternative to biologics. Choice of agent is shaped by comorbidities — IL-17 inhibitors are avoided in inflammatory bowel disease (paradoxical worsening); TNF inhibitors are reasonable in concurrent psoriatic arthritis or IBD; IL-23 inhibitors have favorable long-term safety. Treat-to-target — typically aiming for BSA ≤1% or PASI 90 — replaces older symptom-based goals. All severe psoriasis warrants cardiovascular and metabolic screening and aggressive risk-factor management.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical corticosteroids (mid- to ultra-potent: clobetasol 0.05%, betamethasone dipropionate 0.05%)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for mild-to-moderate plaque psoriasis. Applied once or twice daily for 2-4 weeks for flare control, then tapered or rotated with non-steroid agents.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: PASI 75 in ~50-60% within 4-8 weeks for mild disease; long-term continuous use limited by skin atrophy and tachyphylaxis.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical vitamin D analogues (calcipotriene 0.005%; calcitriol 3 μg/g)
medicalTreatments.description: Modulates keratinocyte differentiation and proliferation. Often combined with topical corticosteroids in fixed combination foams/creams for greater efficacy and less steroid exposure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: PASI 75 in ~30-50% as monotherapy at 8 weeks; combination with betamethasone roughly doubles response.

medicalTreatments.name: Narrowband UVB phototherapy (311-313 nm), 2-3 sessions per week
medicalTreatments.description: Highly effective for moderate plaque or guttate psoriasis. Suppresses cutaneous T-cell activity and induces apoptosis of pathogenic immune cells.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: PASI 75 in 60-75% by week 8-12; remission duration averages 3-6 months after a successful course.

medicalTreatments.name: Methotrexate 15-25 mg weekly plus folic acid 1 mg daily (off-treatment day for folic acid is 5 mg the day after MTX)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-folate immunomodulator. Useful for moderate-to-severe psoriasis and concurrent psoriatic arthritis. Requires LFT and CBC monitoring; avoid in pregnancy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: PASI 75 in ~40% by 16 weeks (CHAMPION trial showed 36% PASI 75).

medicalTreatments.name: Apremilast (PDE-4 inhibitor) 30 mg twice daily
medicalTreatments.description: Oral systemic agent with no required lab monitoring. Reasonable for moderate disease, particularly when biologics are unsuitable or unavailable. Gastrointestinal side effects and headache are common in the first month.

medicalTreatments.name: Adalimumab 80 mg loading, then 40 mg every 2 weeks (TNF inhibitor)
medicalTreatments.description: First-generation biologic with broad efficacy. Particularly useful when both skin and psoriatic arthritis are active. Avoid in active infection, latent TB (treat first), and decompensated heart failure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: PASI 75 in ~70% and PASI 90 in ~45% at week 16 (CHAMPION, REVEAL).

medicalTreatments.name: Secukinumab 300 mg SC weekly x 5, then every 4 weeks (IL-17A inhibitor)
medicalTreatments.description: Selective IL-17A blockade. Rapid skin clearance. Avoid in inflammatory bowel disease due to possible worsening.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: PASI 90 in ~55-70% and PASI 100 in ~25-40% at week 16 (ERASURE, FIXTURE).

medicalTreatments.name: Ixekizumab 160 mg loading, then 80 mg every 2 weeks x 12, then every 4 weeks (IL-17A)
medicalTreatments.description: Highly effective IL-17A inhibitor with very rapid onset. Used for moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis and psoriatic arthritis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: PASI 90 in ~70% and PASI 100 in ~40% at week 12 (UNCOVER-1/2/3).

medicalTreatments.name: Guselkumab 100 mg SC at weeks 0, 4, then every 8 weeks (IL-23p19 inhibitor)
medicalTreatments.description: Highly selective IL-23 inhibition with infrequent dosing. Favorable long-term safety profile and durable response.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: PASI 90 in ~70-80% at week 16 and ~80-85% maintained at week 48 (VOYAGE-1, VOYAGE-2).

medicalTreatments.name: Risankizumab 150 mg SC at weeks 0, 4, then every 12 weeks (IL-23p19)
medicalTreatments.description: IL-23p19 inhibitor with the longest dosing interval among biologics. Frequently the first-line biologic for new starts at many centers.

medicalTreatments.name: Bimekizumab 320 mg SC every 4 weeks x 4, then every 8 weeks (IL-17A and IL-17F)
medicalTreatments.description: Dual IL-17A/F inhibitor with the highest reported skin-clearance rates. Increased risk of mild oral candidiasis given depth of IL-17 blockade.

medicalTreatments.name: Deucravacitinib 6 mg PO daily (TYK2 inhibitor)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral selective allosteric TYK2 inhibitor; an alternative to injection-based therapy. No black-box JAK warnings.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: PASI 75 in ~58% and PASI 90 in ~31% at week 16 (POETYK PSO-1, POETYK PSO-2).

alternativeTreatments.name: Mediterranean dietary pattern with weight loss in overweight patients
alternativeTreatments.description: Caloric restriction with Mediterranean-style eating reduces psoriasis severity in overweight patients independent of medical therapy; PASI improvement of 30-50% has been reported in randomized trials of weight loss in BMI ≥30 patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dead Sea climatotherapy or saltwater bathing plus UVB
alternativeTreatments.description: Balneo-phototherapy (saltwater baths + UVB) has multiple RCT evidence of improved efficacy over UVB alone in moderate plaque psoriasis.

alternativeTreatments.name: Coal tar 5-10% ointment or shampoo (still useful adjunct, especially scalp)
alternativeTreatments.description: Inexpensive option with decades of evidence for mild-to-moderate plaque and scalp psoriasis. Cosmetic acceptability limits use.

whySeeSpecialist: Refer to a dermatologist for any new suspected psoriasis diagnosis, for plaque psoriasis involving more than 3-5% body surface area, for involvement of the scalp, face, palms, soles, nails, or genitals, for failure of over-the-counter or first-line prescription topicals after 4 weeks, and for any pustular or erythrodermic presentation (urgent). Refer to a rheumatologist for joint pain, dactylitis, enthesitis, or morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes in a patient with skin psoriasis.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a board-certified dermatologist comfortable with phototherapy, systemic agents, and the full biologic armamentarium. For complex or treatment-resistant disease, seek a dermatologist affiliated with an academic medical center or designated psoriasis center of excellence (NPF-recognized centers in the US). For psoriatic arthritis, a rheumatologist with expertise in spondyloarthropathies is preferred; integrated psoriasis-PsA clinics deliver the best outcomes.
hospitalCriteria: Dedicated psoriasis or general dermatology service with phototherapy unit (NBUVB, excimer)
Access to current biologic and oral systemic agents through specialty pharmacy partnerships
Integrated rheumatology for psoriatic arthritis co-management
On-site infusion center for infusion-based biologics (infliximab) and IV induction regimens
Mental-health support given the elevated depression and suicidality risk in psoriasis
preventionStrategies: Maintain a healthy weight — BMI in the normal range lowers psoriasis severity and improves response to systemic and biologic therapy.
Stop smoking — palmoplantar pustulosis in particular is closely tied to tobacco; severity drops over 6-12 months after cessation.
Limit alcohol — heavy use worsens disease and raises hepatotoxicity risk on methotrexate.
Manage stress with structured techniques (CBT, mindfulness-based stress reduction); stress is a documented flare trigger.
Treat streptococcal infections promptly — particularly important in patients with a history of guttate psoriasis.
Review medications periodically with your dermatologist — lithium, beta-blockers, antimalarials, and NSAIDs can worsen disease.
lifestyleModifications: Apply a fragrance-free thick emollient (cream or ointment, not lotion) at least twice daily, especially within 3 minutes of bathing.
Use lukewarm rather than hot showers; minimize harsh soaps and exfoliants.
Treat scalp disease with medicated shampoos (salicylic acid, tar, ketoconazole, clobetasol) left in contact for 5-10 minutes before rinsing.
Consider a humidifier in dry winter months to reduce skin cracking and flares.
Avoid scratching, picking, or aggressive exfoliation — the Koebner phenomenon means trauma generates new plaques.
Use mineral-based sunscreen and avoid sunburn; controlled sun exposure helps but burns worsen disease.
Get 7-9 hours of sleep — sleep restriction raises pro-inflammatory cytokines and worsens severity.
Engage with a patient organization (National Psoriasis Foundation, Psoriasis Association UK) for community and self-management resources.

recommended: Mediterranean dietary pattern (vegetables, fruits, whole grains, legumes, olive oil, fish) — observational data link adherence to lower severity
Adequate omega-3 fatty acids from fatty fish (salmon, mackerel, sardines) 2-3 times weekly
Achieve 5-10% body-weight loss if BMI is 30 or higher — measurably improves PASI in randomized trials
Adequate vitamin D (sunlight or supplementation if deficient) — observational link to severity
Plenty of water; mild dehydration worsens skin barrier function
avoid: Excess alcohol (more than 14 drinks per week in men, 7 in women) — worsens disease and limits methotrexate options
Highly processed and ultra-processed foods, which are associated with metabolic syndrome and may worsen inflammation
Excessive saturated fat from processed meats — observational link to severity
Sugary beverages and added sugars beyond occasional intake
Crash diets and severe caloric restriction during a flare — can trigger pustular variants

exerciseGuidelines: Aim for 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic activity per week plus two resistance sessions, the standard adult recommendation. Exercise helps with weight management and reduces systemic inflammation. Avoid tight-fitting workout clothes and shower promptly afterward to reduce sweat-induced irritation in flexural plaques. Public-pool swimming can flare disease for some patients due to chlorine; saltwater swimming is generally well tolerated.
dailyManagement: Apply topicals as prescribed and at the recommended times (vitamin D analogues are sometimes destabilized by potent steroids if applied together — confirm sequencing with your clinician).
Moisturize at least twice daily with a fragrance-free thick emollient
Track triggers in a brief journal — infections, stress, new medications, season, alcohol — for the first 3 months
Maintain biologic injection schedule precisely; missed doses reduce efficacy and risk antidrug antibody formation
Monitor for joint symptoms and report any new morning stiffness, swelling, or dactylitis promptly
Get periodic cardiovascular and metabolic screening — blood pressure, lipids, glucose — at least yearly in moderate-to-severe disease
prognosis: Psoriasis is a chronic relapsing disease without a definitive cure, but modern therapy has transformed outcomes. With biologic therapy, 60-85% of moderate-to-severe patients now achieve PASI 90 (90% improvement) within 16 weeks, and roughly 40-60% achieve complete skin clearance (PASI 100) with the newest IL-17A/F and IL-23 inhibitors. Remission durations of 6-12 months between courses are common with phototherapy alone, and continuous biologic therapy can maintain near-clearance indefinitely in responders. Psoriatic arthritis develops in ~30% of skin-disease patients, typically 5-10 years after skin onset, and early treatment with disease-modifying therapy can prevent joint erosions; nonetheless, untreated PsA can produce permanent joint damage in 40-60% of patients. Cardiovascular mortality is elevated by roughly 50-70% in severe psoriasis; aggressive treatment of skin disease and cardiometabolic risk factors narrows this gap. Mental-health comorbidity is substantial — depression affects up to 25% and the suicide risk is roughly 1.5-2× the general population — and integrated care that addresses mood and stress materially improves outcomes.
recoveryTimeline: Topical regimens for mild-to-moderate plaque psoriasis typically begin to soften plaques within 2-3 weeks and reach maximum effect by 6-8 weeks. Phototherapy schedules over 8-12 weeks induce remission in most responders, often lasting 3-6 months. Methotrexate and apremilast reach peak effect at 12-16 weeks. Biologics show first improvements within 2-4 weeks; peak skin clearance with IL-17A/F and IL-23 inhibitors typically occurs by week 16, with maintenance dosing continued indefinitely while response persists.
complications: Psoriatic arthritis — affects 30% of patients and can cause permanent joint damage if not treated early. Screen with PEST/PASE questionnaires at every visit.
Cardiovascular disease — severe psoriasis raises MI risk by ~50-70%; treat lipids, blood pressure, and diabetes aggressively and counsel on smoking cessation.
Inflammatory bowel disease — psoriasis and IBD share pathophysiology; choose biologics carefully (TNF or IL-23 inhibitors are preferred over IL-17 in patients with comorbid IBD).
Depression, anxiety, and suicide risk — screen routinely (PHQ-2/9) and refer to mental-health services as needed.
Uveitis — particularly in psoriatic arthritis. Acute red eye with photophobia warrants urgent ophthalmology evaluation.
Erythrodermic or generalized pustular psoriasis — life-threatening flares producing fluid loss, temperature dysregulation, and high-output cardiac failure; require hospital admission.

supportResources.name: National Psoriasis Foundation (NPF)
supportResources.description: US patient-facing organization with treatment guides, advocacy, financial-assistance referrals, and patient community.

supportResources.name: Psoriasis Association (UK)
supportResources.description: UK-based patient organization with resources for diagnosis, treatment, and support groups.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Dermatology — Psoriasis
supportResources.description: Patient-facing overview with treatment options and lifestyle guidance from the specialty society.

supportResources.name: DermNet — Psoriasis
supportResources.description: Comprehensive image-rich educational resource on psoriasis variants and management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Eczema produces ill-defined, intensely itchy patches that favor flexural areas (antecubital and popliteal fossae) and typically begins in infancy. Psoriasis produces sharply demarcated plaques with thick silvery scale that favor extensor surfaces and tends to begin in adolescence or adulthood. Itch is more intense in eczema; scale is thicker and more silvery in psoriasis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Seborrheic Dermatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Seborrheic dermatitis produces greasy, yellowish scale in sebaceous areas (scalp, eyebrows, nasolabial folds, central chest) with less well-defined borders. Psoriasis produces sharply demarcated plaques with thicker silvery-white scale. Scalp involvement that extends well beyond the hairline favors psoriasis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lichen Planus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lichen planus produces flat-topped violaceous (purple-pink) papules with fine white Wickham striae, often on the wrists, ankles, and oral mucosa. Psoriasis produces raised silvery-scaled erythematous plaques on extensor surfaces. Wickham striae and intense pruritus point to lichen planus.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rosacea
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Rosacea produces facial erythema, telangiectasias, and inflammatory papules and pustules centered on the cheeks, nose, chin, and forehead, typically without scale. Psoriasis on the face is less common and shows the characteristic silvery scale and sharp borders.

confusedWithConditions.name: Psoriatic Arthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Psoriatic arthritis is the inflammatory joint disease associated with psoriasis, not a separate skin condition. About 30% of psoriasis patients develop PsA; in 15% joint disease precedes skin disease, which can delay diagnosis. PsA presents with dactylitis, enthesitis, asymmetric oligoarthritis, or DIP-joint arthritis.

relatedConditions.name: Psoriatic Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Develops in 30% of psoriasis patients and warrants early screening at every dermatology visit.

relatedConditions.name: Eczema
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common differential diagnosis and a frequently comorbid skin condition.

relatedConditions.name: Rosacea
relatedConditions.relevance: Another chronic inflammatory skin disease seen by dermatologists; sometimes confused with facial psoriasis.

relatedConditions.name: Lichen Planus
relatedConditions.relevance: Differential consideration for scaly papulosquamous eruptions, especially on flexor surfaces.

relatedConditions.name: Seborrheic Dermatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: The closest mimic of scalp and facial psoriasis; overlap presentation is termed sebopsoriasis.

faqs.question: What does psoriasis look like?
faqs.answer: Classic plaque psoriasis appears as well-demarcated, raised red patches covered with thick silvery-white scale, typically symmetric on the elbows, knees, scalp, and lower back. The patches are usually round or oval, can range from coin-sized to large continuous plaques, and may itch, burn, or crack. Nail pitting and scalp scaling beyond the hairline are common.

faqs.question: Is psoriasis contagious?
faqs.answer: No. Psoriasis is an immune-mediated genetic disease, not an infection. You cannot pass it to others through skin contact, sharing clothes, or any other means. Genetic susceptibility is responsible for clustering in families.

faqs.question: What is the difference between psoriasis and eczema?
faqs.answer: Eczema causes intensely itchy, ill-defined patches that favor flexural areas (insides of elbows and knees) and usually starts in childhood. Psoriasis causes sharply demarcated plaques with thick silvery scale that favor extensor surfaces and most often starts in adolescence or adulthood. The scale is thicker and more silvery in psoriasis; itch dominates eczema.

faqs.question: Can psoriasis be cured?
faqs.answer: There is no permanent cure for psoriasis, but it can be controlled extremely well. Modern biologic therapies achieve 90-100% skin clearance (PASI 90 or PASI 100) in 60-85% of moderate-to-severe patients within 16 weeks, and continuous therapy maintains near-clearance for many years.

faqs.question: What triggers psoriasis flares?
faqs.answer: Common triggers include streptococcal throat infections, stress, skin trauma (the Koebner phenomenon), cold dry weather, smoking, alcohol, certain medications (beta-blockers, lithium, antimalarials, NSAIDs), and abrupt withdrawal of systemic steroids. Each patient often identifies a personal set of triggers over time.

faqs.question: Can psoriasis affect joints?
faqs.answer: Yes. About 30% of psoriasis patients develop psoriatic arthritis, usually 5-10 years after skin onset but sometimes before. It causes morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes, swollen sausage-shaped fingers (dactylitis), tenderness where tendons attach to bone (enthesitis), and asymmetric joint pain. Early rheumatology referral helps prevent permanent joint damage.

faqs.question: What are biologics for psoriasis?
faqs.answer: Biologics are injectable proteins that block specific immune signals driving psoriasis. The most effective newer classes target IL-17 (secukinumab, ixekizumab, brodalumab, bimekizumab) or IL-23 (guselkumab, risankizumab, tildrakizumab). They achieve PASI 90 in 60-85% of moderate-to-severe patients at week 16, far higher than older systemic agents.

faqs.question: Is psoriasis hereditary?
faqs.answer: Yes, substantially. Heritability is roughly 60-70%, and the strongest risk allele is HLA-Cw0602. If one parent has psoriasis the child has about a 16% lifetime risk; if both parents are affected the risk approaches 50%. Environmental triggers determine when and how severely it manifests.

faqs.question: Does diet affect psoriasis?
faqs.answer: Diet is not a primary cause, but obesity worsens severity and reduces response to treatment. Weight loss of 5-10% measurably improves skin disease in overweight patients. Mediterranean-style eating patterns and limiting heavy alcohol intake are associated with better disease control.

faqs.question: Why is my scalp psoriasis so itchy?
faqs.answer: Scalp psoriasis carries thicker scale than skin elsewhere because the scalp grows hair-related keratinocytes more rapidly, and the scale traps inflammatory mediators close to nerve endings. Medicated shampoos containing salicylic acid, coal tar, or ketoconazole reduce scale, and topical clobetasol or calcipotriene solutions or foams reduce itch and inflammation.

faqs.question: Can children get psoriasis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Roughly one-third of psoriasis cases begin before age 20, with a peak between ages 15-20. Guttate psoriasis after streptococcal pharyngitis is particularly common in children and adolescents. Pediatric dermatology has dedicated topical, phototherapy, and biologic options.

faqs.question: Is psoriasis linked to heart disease?
faqs.answer: Yes. Severe psoriasis raises cardiovascular event risk by roughly 50-70% versus the general population, driven by shared inflammatory pathways and a higher prevalence of metabolic syndrome. Aggressive treatment of skin disease and cardiometabolic risk factors narrows the gap.

faqs.question: Should I get the COVID-19 vaccine if I have psoriasis?
faqs.answer: Yes. The National Psoriasis Foundation and dermatology societies recommend COVID-19 and other routine vaccinations for psoriasis patients on or off biologics. Live vaccines should be avoided while on most biologics; inactivated and mRNA vaccines are safe and effective.

faqs.question: Does sunlight help psoriasis?
faqs.answer: Controlled UV exposure helps many psoriasis patients, which is why narrowband UVB phototherapy is a mainstay of treatment. Brief regular sun exposure during summer can improve plaques, but sunburn worsens disease through the Koebner phenomenon. Use mineral sunscreen and avoid burning.

faqs.question: What is PASI?
faqs.answer: PASI stands for Psoriasis Area and Severity Index, a 0-72 score that quantifies erythema, induration, scale, and area across four body regions. Treatment responses are reported as PASI 75, PASI 90, or PASI 100 — meaning a 75%, 90%, or 100% reduction from baseline. PASI 90 is the current standard of care benchmark.

faqs.question: Can I drink alcohol with psoriasis?
faqs.answer: Heavy alcohol intake worsens psoriasis severity and reduces treatment response. Moderate intake (up to one drink per day for women, two for men) is acceptable for most patients. Patients on methotrexate should minimize alcohol because of liver-injury risk.

faqs.question: Are biologics safe long-term?
faqs.answer: IL-23 and IL-17 inhibitors have a favorable long-term safety profile in five-year and longer follow-up. Main concerns are infection (particularly upper-respiratory and oral candida with IL-17), reactivation of latent tuberculosis (screen and treat first), and rare paradoxical reactions. Routine monitoring with periodic CBC, LFTs, and clinical assessment is standard.

faqs.question: What is the Koebner phenomenon?
faqs.answer: The Koebner phenomenon is the appearance of new psoriasis plaques at sites of skin trauma — scratches, sunburn, surgical incisions, or tattoos — typically 7-14 days after the injury. Around 25-30% of psoriasis patients show it. Avoiding skin trauma during a flare reduces new lesion formation.

faqs.question: Can stress cause psoriasis flares?
faqs.answer: Yes. Acute stressful life events precede flares in 40-80% of patients, and chronic stress reduces response to treatment. Cognitive behavioral therapy, mindfulness-based stress reduction, and integrated mental-health support measurably improve disease control.

faqs.question: Will my nails be permanently damaged?
faqs.answer: Nail changes in psoriasis can be reversed with effective treatment, but improvement is slow because fingernails grow only 3 mm per month. Biologics targeting IL-17 or IL-23 are highly effective for nail psoriasis, with substantial improvement typically visible by 6 months. Long-standing severe nail disease may leave residual changes.

faqs.question: Is psoriasis an autoimmune disease?
faqs.answer: Psoriasis is classified as an immune-mediated inflammatory disease rather than a classic autoimmune disease, because no clearly defined self-antigen has been confirmed. The dominant mechanism is dysregulation of the IL-23/Th17 immune axis. The distinction does not change how psoriasis is treated.

metaDescription: Psoriasis: thick silvery plaques driven by IL-23 and IL-17. Symptoms, biologics, phototherapy, and lifestyle care reviewed by our medical board.
